subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Year 7 Science, and welcome to one of the most amazing ideas in all of biology. In this unit you are going to discover something that is invisible to your eyes yet present everywhere around you and even inside you at this very moment. Every living thing you have ever seen is built from tiny units called cells. The camel resting in the shade of an acacia tree, the tilapia swimming in the Jubba River, the mango ripening in your garden, the goat chewing its cud, and you yourself as you read these words are all made of cells. A cell is the smallest part of a living thing that is still alive on its own. This single idea is one of the great discoveries of science, and by the end of this unit you will understand it deeply.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 7 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: State the cell theory — that all living things are made of cells, and that cells come from other cells.
Identify the main parts of an animal cell and describe what each part does.
Identify the main parts of a plant cell and explain the extra parts that plants have.
Compare plant and animal cells, listing what they share and how they differ.
Explain how a light microscope is used to see cells, and why microscopes were needed to discover them.
Describe several specialised cells and connect each one's shape to the job it does.
Put the levels of organisation in order: cells to tissues to organs to organ systems to the whole organism.
Describe unicellular organisms such as bacteria and explain how one cell can carry out all of life's functions.

Before we explore, let us meet the words scientists use. Read this table slowly. You do not need to memorise every word today — you will meet them again and again as the unit goes on, and each one will become familiar.
The smallest unit of a living thing that is still alive.

concepts.title: Think about a large building in Mogadishu or Hargeisa. From…
concepts.explanation: Think about a large building in Mogadishu or Hargeisa. From far away it looks like one solid object, but as you come closer you notice it is made of thousands of individual bricks, each one placed carefully to build the whole. Living things are like this too. Your body is not one single piece of material; it is built from roughly thirty trillion cells working together. That number is so large it is difficult to imagine. If you tried to count your cells one every second, without ever stopping to sleep or eat, it…
concepts.example: Cells matter to your everyday life in ways you may never have noticed. When a farmer near Garissa wonders why his crops wilt in the dry season, the answer lies inside the plant cells. When a doctor in Nairobi treats a…

concepts.title: Cells matter to your everyday life in ways you may never…
concepts.explanation: Cells matter to your everyday life in ways you may never have noticed. When a farmer near Garissa wonders why his crops wilt in the dry season, the answer lies inside the plant cells. When a doctor in Nairobi treats a child for malaria, she is fighting a battle happening between blood cells and tiny invaders. When your grandmother tells you to boil water from the well before drinking it, she is protecting you from single-celled organisms too small to see. Understanding cells helps us understand health, food,…

explorations.title: Looking at Onion-Skin Cells
explorations.context: To observe real plant cells from an onion using a magnifying glass and to draw what you see.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down what you expect. Do you think the onion will be made of many tiny separate units, or one smooth piece? Write your prediction as a full sentence beginning, "I predict that..."
explorations.answer: Did your observation match your prediction? Explain. The onion cells are shaped like neat boxes. Which part of a plant cell gives them this shape? Onion-skin cells have no chloroplasts. Why does that make sense for a part of the plant that grows underground, hidden from sunlight?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe real plant cells from an onion using a magnifying glass and to draw what you see.

explorations.title: Building a Model Cell from Household Items
explorations.context: To build a model of a plant or animal cell using everyday items, and to use the model to explain what each part does.
explorations.prompt: A model helps us understand things that are too small to see. Before you start, write one sentence about what you think will be the hardest cell part to represent well, and why.
explorations.answer: Which household item was the best match for its cell part? Explain why. Every model has limits. Name one way your model is NOT like a real cell. If you built a plant cell, how did you show that a real cell wall is stiff while a membrane is soft?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a model of a plant or animal cell using everyday items, and to use the model to explain what each part does.

explorations.title: The Egg as a Giant Model Cell
explorations.context: To use a single raw egg as a giant model of an animal cell and to connect its parts to the real parts of a cell.
explorations.prompt: An egg is one of the largest single cells you will ever hold. Predict which part of the egg you think acts like the nucleus, and write down your reason.
explorations.answer: Why is the yolk a good model for the nucleus? Think about its position and how it stands out. A real cell also has mitochondria, which the egg does not clearly show. What does this tell you about the limits of using an egg as a model? The egg is an animal cell model. Name two parts you would have…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To use a single raw egg as a giant model of an animal cell and to connect its parts to the real parts of a cell.

explorations.title: Why Plants Wilt — The Vacuole in Action
explorations.context: To observe how water moving in and out of plant cells makes a vegetable go firm or floppy, showing the job of the sap vacuole.
explorations.prompt: Predict what will happen to a fresh stick of carrot, potato, or sukuma-wiki stalk left in salty water compared with one left in plain water. Write both predictions.
explorations.answer: Which stick became floppy? Which stayed firm or got firmer? In the salty water, water moved OUT of the cells and their vacuoles shrank. How does this explain why that stick went floppy? How does this investigation help explain why a plant wilts in dry weather, and why salty soil is bad for crops?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe how water moving in and out of plant cells makes a vegetable go firm or floppy, showing the job of the sap vacuole.

methods.title: Looking at Onion-Skin Cells
methods.example: To observe real plant cells from an onion using a magnifying glass and to draw what you see.
steps: Ask an adult to help you cut the onion into quarters.
Look closely between the fleshy layers. You will see a thin, clear, papery skin. Gently peel off a small piece of this skin — it should be almost see-through.
Lay the thin skin flat on a piece of white paper. Try not to let it fold or wrinkle.
Hold the magnifying glass above the skin and move it slowly up and down until the skin comes into sharp focus.
Look for a repeating pattern of tiny brick-shaped rooms. These are the onion cells. If you have iodine or strong tea, dab a little on the skin; the stain makes the cells easier to see.

methods.title: Building a Model Cell from Household Items
methods.example: To build a model of a plant or animal cell using everyday items, and to use the model to explain what each part does.
steps: Decide whether you are modelling a plant cell or an animal cell. For a plant cell use a firm bowl as the cell wall; for an animal cell use a soft plastic bag as the flexible membrane.
Half-fill your container with the rice, jelly, or porridge. This is your cytoplasm.
Push the round fruit or bottle cap into the middle. This is your nucleus, the control centre.
Scatter in the beans or grains. These are the mitochondria, the power stations.
If you are making a plant cell, add the green scraps as chloroplasts and place the small water sachet in the centre as the sap vacuole.

methods.title: The Egg as a Giant Model Cell
methods.example: To use a single raw egg as a giant model of an animal cell and to connect its parts to the real parts of a cell.
steps: Wash your hands, then carefully crack the raw egg onto the clear plate. Try not to break the yolk.
Look at the whole egg on the plate. The thin skin holding it in shape is like the cell membrane.
The clear, thick, jelly-like part around the yolk is the egg white. This is like the cytoplasm of a cell.
The round yellow yolk sitting in the middle is like the nucleus — a large, important structure that stands out clearly in the centre.
Draw the egg on your plate and label the three parts with the cell parts they represent: membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus.

methods.title: Why Plants Wilt — The Vacuole in Action
methods.example: To observe how water moving in and out of plant cells makes a vegetable go firm or floppy, showing the job of the sap vacuole.
steps: With an adult's help, cut two sticks of vegetable of about the same size. Bend each one gently to feel how firm it is, and note this down.
Fill one cup with plain water. Fill the other with water and stir in several spoonfuls of salt until no more dissolves.
Put one vegetable stick in each cup, making sure it is covered. Label which cup is which.
Leave them for at least two hours, or overnight if you can.
Take out both sticks. Bend each one again and feel the difference. Record whether each stick is firm or floppy.

workedExamples.title: Working Out Magnification
workedExamples.prompt: In science we often need to calculate how much bigger a microscope makes things appear. The total magnification is found by multiplying the magnification of the eyepiece by the magnification of the objective lens you are using. The rule is written like this:
workedExamples.solution: Total magnification = eyepiece magnification x objective magnification Let us work through an example together, step by step, the way you should always show your working. Question: Amina is looking at an onion-skin slide. Her eyepiece magnifies ten times (written 10x) and the objective lens she has chosen magnifies forty times (40x). What is the total magnification? Step 1 — Write down what you know. Eyepiece = 10x. Objective = 40x.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Which part controls what enters and leaves the cell? (a) nucleus (b) cytoplasm (c) cell membrane (d) mitochondrion
workedExamples.solution: 2 c — cell membrane The membrane is selectively permeable, choosing what passes in and out.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: The control centre of the cell is the: (a) vacuole (b) chloroplast (c) cell wall (d) nucleus
workedExamples.solution: 3 d — nucleus The nucleus holds the instructions and directs all the cell's activities.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Which part is found in plant cells but NOT animal cells? (a) nucleus (b) cell wall (c) cell membrane (d) cytoplasm
workedExamples.solution: 4 b — cell wall Only plant cells have a cellulose cell wall; the others are in both cell types.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Chloroplasts are green because they contain: (a) cellulose (b) cytoplasm (c) chlorophyll (d) water
workedExamples.solution: 5 c — chlorophyll Chlorophyll is the green pigment inside chloroplasts that traps sunlight.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Energy is released from food inside the: (a) mitochondria (b) nucleus (c) vacuole (d) cell wall
workedExamples.solution: 6 a — mitochondria Respiration releases energy from food inside the mitochondria.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: The jelly-like material that fills the cell is the: (a) membrane (b) cytoplasm (c) nucleus (d) chloroplast
workedExamples.solution: 7 b — cytoplasm The cytoplasm is the jelly where many reactions happen.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: A group of similar cells working together is called: (a) an organ (b) an organism (c) a tissue (d) an organ system
workedExamples.solution: 8 c — a tissue Similar cells working together form a tissue, such as muscle tissue.

practice.prompt: The smallest unit of a living thing that is still alive is called: (a) an organ (b) a cell (c) a tissue (d) a molecule
practice.answer: 1 b — a cell A cell is the smallest unit that is still alive; organs and tissues are made OF cells.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which part controls what enters and leaves the cell? (a) nucleus (b) cytoplasm (c) cell membrane (d) mitochondrion
practice.answer: 2 c — cell membrane The membrane is selectively permeable, choosing what passes in and out.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The control centre of the cell is the: (a) vacuole (b) chloroplast (c) cell wall (d) nucleus
practice.answer: 3 d — nucleus The nucleus holds the instructions and directs all the cell's activities.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which part is found in plant cells but NOT animal cells? (a) nucleus (b) cell wall (c) cell membrane (d) cytoplasm
practice.answer: 4 b — cell wall Only plant cells have a cellulose cell wall; the others are in both cell types.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Chloroplasts are green because they contain: (a) cellulose (b) cytoplasm (c) chlorophyll (d) water
practice.answer: 5 c — chlorophyll Chlorophyll is the green pigment inside chloroplasts that traps sunlight.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Energy is released from food inside the: (a) mitochondria (b) nucleus (c) vacuole (d) cell wall
practice.answer: 6 a — mitochondria Respiration releases energy from food inside the mitochondria.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The jelly-like material that fills the cell is the: (a) membrane (b) cytoplasm (c) nucleus (d) chloroplast
practice.answer: 7 b — cytoplasm The cytoplasm is the jelly where many reactions happen.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A group of similar cells working together is called: (a) an organ (b) an organism (c) a tissue (d) an organ system
practice.answer: 8 c — a tissue Similar cells working together form a tissue, such as muscle tissue.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a unicellular organism? (a) a mango tree (b) a camel (c) a bacterium (d) a goat
practice.answer: 9 c — a bacterium A bacterium is a single-celled (unicellular) organism; the others are multicellular.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Red blood cells are specialised to: (a) carry oxygen (b) make food (c) send messages (d) absorb water
practice.answer: 10 a — carry oxygen Red blood cells are biconcave and lose their nucleus to carry more oxygen.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A microscope's total magnification is found by: (a) adding the two lenses (b) multiplying eyepiece by objective (c) subtracting them (d) dividing them
practice.answer: 11 b — multiply eyepiece by objective Total magnification = eyepiece x objective (e.g. 10 x 40 = 400).
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The correct order from smallest to largest is: (a) organ, tissue, cell, organism (b) cell, tissue, organ, organism (c) tissue, cell, organism, organ (d) organism, organ, tissue, cell
practice.answer: 12 b — cell, tissue, organ, organism This is the correct order of organisation from small to large.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Looking at Onion-Skin Cells
activities.materials: One onion; A table knife (use with an adult's help); A magnifying glass — the stronger the better; A clear drinking glass and some water; A pinch of iodine from a first-aid kit, OR strong tea, as a stain (optional); White paper and a pencil for drawing
steps: Ask an adult to help you cut the onion into quarters.
Look closely between the fleshy layers. You will see a thin, clear, papery skin. Gently peel off a small piece of this skin — it should be almost see-through.
Lay the thin skin flat on a piece of white paper. Try not to let it fold or wrinkle.
Hold the magnifying glass above the skin and move it slowly up and down until the skin comes into sharp focus.
Look for a repeating pattern of tiny brick-shaped rooms. These are the onion cells. If you have iodine or strong tea, dab a little on the skin; the stain makes the cells easier to see.

activities.title: Building a Model Cell from Household Items
activities.materials: A clear plastic bag or a shallow bowl (to be the cell membrane or cell wall); Cooked rice, jelly, or a thick porridge (to be the cytoplasm); A small round fruit such as a date, a grape, or a bottle cap (to be the nucleus); A few beans or grains (to be the mitochondria); Green leaves or small green…
steps: Decide whether you are modelling a plant cell or an animal cell. For a plant cell use a firm bowl as the cell wall; for an animal cell use a soft plastic bag as the flexible membrane.
Half-fill your container with the rice, jelly, or porridge. This is your cytoplasm.
Push the round fruit or bottle cap into the middle. This is your nucleus, the control centre.
Scatter in the beans or grains. These are the mitochondria, the power stations.
If you are making a plant cell, add the green scraps as chloroplasts and place the small water sachet in the centre as the sap vacuole.

activities.title: The Egg as a Giant Model Cell
activities.materials: One raw egg; A clear bowl or plate; An adult to supervise (raw egg must be handled carefully)
steps: Wash your hands, then carefully crack the raw egg onto the clear plate. Try not to break the yolk.
Look at the whole egg on the plate. The thin skin holding it in shape is like the cell membrane.
The clear, thick, jelly-like part around the yolk is the egg white. This is like the cytoplasm of a cell.
The round yellow yolk sitting in the middle is like the nucleus — a large, important structure that stands out clearly in the centre.
Draw the egg on your plate and label the three parts with the cell parts they represent: membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus.

activities.title: Why Plants Wilt — The Vacuole in Action
activities.materials: Two fresh sticks cut from a carrot, potato, or firm vegetable stalk (similar size); Two cups or glasses; Plain water; A few spoonfuls of salt; A table knife and an adult's help for cutting
steps: With an adult's help, cut two sticks of vegetable of about the same size. Bend each one gently to feel how firm it is, and note this down.
Fill one cup with plain water. Fill the other with water and stir in several spoonfuls of salt until no more dissolves.
Put one vegetable stick in each cup, making sure it is covered. Label which cup is which.
Leave them for at least two hours, or overnight if you can.
Take out both sticks. Bend each one again and feel the difference. Record whether each stick is firm or floppy.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: Every large and complex living thing began as a single cell and is built from cells that cooperate in astonishing order. When you next look at a leaf, a drop of water, or the back of your own hand, remember the hidden world of tiny living units working together inside.

terms: Cell
The smallest unit of a living thing that is still alive.
Cell membrane
Thin flexible layer around every cell; controls what enters and leaves.
Cytoplasm
Jelly-like material filling the cell where reactions happen.
Nucleus
The control centre; holds the genetic instructions.
Cell wall
Strong outer layer of cellulose, found only in plant cells.
Cellulose
The tough material a plant cell wall is made from.
Chloroplast
Green organelle in plant cells; site of photosynthesis.
Chlorophyll
The green pigment inside chloroplasts that traps sunlight.
Mitochondrion
Organelle where energy is released from food (respiration).
Sap vacuole
Large fluid space in plant cells; stores water, keeps the cell firm.
Photosynthesis
Making food from sunlight, water and carbon dioxide inside chloroplasts.
Respiration
Releasing energy from food inside the mitochondria.
commonMistakes: Mixing up cell wall and membrane.
Thinking big animals have big cells.
Cell size is similar across animals.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you know to answer these questions set in real situations.
realProblems.answer: Untreated well water can contain harmful unicellular organisms (such as certain bacteria) that are too small to see and can cause illness. Boiling uses heat to destroy these living cells, making the water safe. Full marks link the tiny organisms to the effect of heat.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Untreated well water can contain harmful unicellular organisms (such as certain bacteria) that are too small to see and can cause illness. Boiling uses heat to destroy these living cells, making the water safe. Full marks link the tiny organisms to the effect of heat.

realProblems.prompt: Halima's grandmother insists that well water be boiled before drinking. Using the idea of unicellular organisms, explain the science behind this rule.
realProblems.answer: During the hot day the plant loses water faster than its roots can replace it, so the sap vacuoles shrink and the cells go floppy — the plant wilts. At night it is cooler, less water is lost, the roots refill the cells, the vacuoles swell again, and the plant becomes firm and recovers.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: During the hot day the plant loses water faster than its roots can replace it, so the sap vacuoles shrink and the cells go floppy — the plant wilts. At night it is cooler, less water is lost, the roots refill the cells, the vacuoles swell again, and the plant becomes firm and recovers.

realProblems.prompt: A farmer near Berbera notices his tomato plants wilt badly at midday but recover by evening. Explain what is happening inside the plant cells during the day and at night.
realProblems.answer: Helpful bacteria (unicellular organisms) feed on the milk and change it, producing the sour tangy taste. These particular bacteria are not harmful; they are the useful kind, like those in many fermented foods.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Helpful bacteria (unicellular organisms) feed on the milk and change it, producing the sour tangy taste. These particular bacteria are not harmful; they are the useful kind, like those in many fermented foods.

realProblems.prompt: Fermented camel milk has a sour, tangy taste. Explain what kind of tiny living things cause this, and say whether they are harmful.
realProblems.answer: The camel stores the water it drinks inside its body cells. Kept plump and full, these cells keep working normally even during long journeys with no wells, so the camel can survive many days of drought. (This links cell water storage to survival.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The camel stores the water it drinks inside its body cells. Kept plump and full, these cells keep working normally even during long journeys with no wells, so the camel can survive many days of drought. (This links cell water storage to survival.)

realProblems.prompt: A camel drinks over a hundred litres of water at a well after days without any. Explain how storing water in its cells helps it survive long journeys through dry land.
realProblems.answer: They are likely to be unicellular organisms such as bacteria or other single-celled pond life. A single one can survive alone because that one cell carries out every life process itself — taking in food, releasing energy, removing waste, responding, and reproducing.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: They are likely to be unicellular organisms such as bacteria or other single-celled pond life. A single one can survive alone because that one cell carries out every life process itself — taking in food, releasing energy, removing waste, responding, and reproducing.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf looks at pond water under a magnifying glass and sees tiny moving shapes. What are these likely to be, and why can a single one of them stay alive on its own?
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State the three parts of the cell theory.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The cell theory: (1) all living things are made of one or more cells; (2) the cell is the basic unit of life; (3) all cells come from existing cells. Marks for all three parts.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the four parts found in BOTH plant and animal cells.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Found in both: cell membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus, and mitochondria. These do jobs every living cell needs.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three extra parts found ONLY in plant cells.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Only in plant cells: cell wall, chloroplasts, and a large sap vacuole.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why animal cells do not need chloroplasts.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Animal cells do not have chloroplasts because animals do not make their own food from sunlight; they get energy by eating plants or other animals, so they have no need to photosynthesise.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does a plant cell have a fixed box shape while an animal cell is rounded?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The plant cell has a rigid cell wall made of cellulose that holds a fixed box shape, while an animal cell has only a soft flexible membrane, so it stays rounded and can change shape.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why a hard-working muscle cell contains many mitochondria.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A muscle cell does hard work such as movement, which needs a lot of energy. Mitochondria release energy, so the more energy a cell needs, the more mitochondria it contains.

questions.question: The smallest unit of a living thing that is still alive is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a cell A cell is the smallest unit that is still alive; organs and tissues are made OF cells.

questions.question: Which part controls what enters and leaves the cell?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: cell membrane The membrane is selectively permeable, choosing what passes in and out.

questions.question: The control centre of the cell is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: nucleus The nucleus holds the instructions and directs all the cell's activities.

questions.question: Which part is found in plant cells but NOT animal cells?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: cell wall Only plant cells have a cellulose cell wall; the others are in both cell types.

questions.question: Chloroplasts are green because they contain:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: chlorophyll Chlorophyll is the green pigment inside chloroplasts that traps sunlight.

questions.question: Energy is released from food inside the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: mitochondria Respiration releases energy from food inside the mitochondria.

questions.question: The jelly-like material that fills the cell is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: cytoplasm The cytoplasm is the jelly where many reactions happen.

questions.question: A group of similar cells working together is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a tissue Similar cells working together form a tissue, such as muscle tissue.

questions.question: Which of these is a unicellular organism?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a bacterium A bacterium is a single-celled (unicellular) organism; the others are multicellular.

questions.question: Red blood cells are specialised to:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: carry oxygen Red blood cells are biconcave and lose their nucleus to carry more oxygen.

questions.question: A microscope's total magnification is found by:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: multiply eyepiece by objective Total magnification = eyepiece x objective (e.g. 10 x 40 = 400).

questions.question: The correct order from smallest to largest is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: cell, tissue, organ, organism This is the correct order of organisation from small to large.

selfAssessment: I can state the cell theory — that all living things are made of cells, and that cells come from other cells.
I can identify the main parts of an animal cell and describe what each part does.
I can identify the main parts of a plant cell and explain the extra parts that plants have.
I can compare plant and animal cells, listing what they share and how they differ.
I can explain how a light microscope is used to see cells, and why microscopes were needed to discover them.
I can describe several specialised cells and connect each one's shape to the job it does.
I can put the levels of organisation in order: cells to tissues to organs to organ systems to the whole organism.
I can describe unicellular organisms such as bacteria and explain how one cell can carry out all of life's functions.
